elements.sent-2411-318: In this short section we introduce an important operation on the blue faces of embedded maps, called the pinching operation.
elements.sent-2411-319: Throughout the paper, it will be repeatedly used to get certain surface cancellations (recall Definition~\ref{def:surface-cancellation ).}
elements.sent-2411-320: We start with a few definitions.
elements.sent-2411-321: We say that a face of an embedded map has disjoint vertices if none of its vertices are identified; see the left-hand side of Figure~\ref{fig-pinching for an example.}

elements.sent-2411-323: Fix a blue face $B$ of $M$ with disjoint vertices and let $v$ be a vertex of $B$.

elements.sent-2411-325: Given any vertex $u\neq v$ on $B$ in the same partite class as $v$, let $\pp_{u,v}$ denote the pinching operation that pinches the vertices $u$ and $v$ together inside $B$.
elements.sent-2411-326: More precisely, one draws a line from $u$ to $v$ through the interior of $B$ and contracts this line to a point splitting $B$ into two blue faces $\pp_{u,v (B)$ that share a vertex (the vertex corresponding to $u$ and $v$).}
elements.sent-2411-327: See the right-hand side of Figure~\ref{fig-pinching for an example.}

elements.sent-2411-330: Similarly, we let $\AM(M, B, v)$ denote the set of all maps that can be obtained from $M$ by the same type of pinchings.

elements.sent-2411-637: Recall that the PPS process is only defined when $\npe(\ell,K)\neq \emptyset$.

elements.sent-2411-640: The key step will be to consider some inverse operations from the PPS process.. Suppose that $\npe(\ell,K)=\emptyset$.
elements.sent-2411-641: If the four sums on the right-hand side of the master loop equation are empty, then we are done.
elements.sent-2411-642: Thus, suppose it is not the case, that is, there exists some splitting or deformation of $\ell$, call it $s'$, such that $\npe(s',K')\neq \emptyset$.
elements.sent-2411-643: We claim that $s'$ cannot be a splitting of $\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-644:

pe(s',K)
eq \emptyset$.. Consider $M'\in 
pe(s',K)$.
elements.sent-2411-645: Then it is simple to see that we can construct a map in $M\in\npe(\ell,K)$ by reversing the PPS process for (positive or negative) splittings.

elements.section-2411-006: Cancellations for sums of embedded maps obtained from pinchings of a blue face
elements.sent-2411-656: The main result of this section is Theorem~\ref{thm:master-sum-blue-faces which establishes some fundamental surface cancellations obtained from pinchings of a blue face.}
elements.sent-2411-657: This result will be one of the main tools that we will use later in Section~\ref{sec: cancellation lemma to prove the Master surface cancellation lemma~\ref{lemma:master-cancellation .}}
elements.sent-2411-658: The rest of this section is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-659: In Section~\ref{sect:seq-pinch , we introduce the new notion of collections of pinchings and state our main result, i.e.\ Theorem~\ref{thm:master-sum-blue-faces .}}
elements.sent-2411-660: Then in Section~\ref{sect:all-pinching we prove a generalization of the Cancellation Lemma~\ref{lemma: single vertex pinching cancellations and establish the first part of Theorem~\ref{thm:master-sum-blue-faces .}}}
elements.sent-2411-661: Finally, in Section~\ref{subsec: Weight of maps with at least one invalid pinching we complete the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:master-sum-blue-faces .}}
elements.subsection-2411-012: Collections of pinchings and a new surface cancellation result
elements.sent-2411-662: Recall from Section~\ref{sect:pinchings (see also Figure~\ref{fig-pinching ) that given two vertices $u$ and $v$ of a blue face $B$ of an embedded map $M\in \npe(s,K)$ of the same partite class, we defined the pinching operation $\pp_{u,v}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-663: Given a collection of pinchings $\left\{\pp_{u_1,v_1 ,\dots,\pp_{u_r,v_r}\right\}$ where each pair $(u_i,v_i)$ is formed by vertices of the same blue face $B$, we denote the operation that sequentially performs them by\footnote{We will shortly clarify (after certain preliminary comments) that the order in which the pinchings are performed is not relevant, this is why we defined $\cpp$ as a set $\{\pp_{u_i,v_i}\}_{i=1 ^r$ and not as a sequence.}}}
elements.sent-2411-664: We stress that we do not require that all pinchings involved in a collection of pinchings are in the same partite class of vertices (see again the example at the top of Figure~\ref{fig-pinching2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-665: Note that some collections of pinchings might not be realizable, as one pinching could preclude another.
elements.sent-2411-666: This occurs when there exists an $i$ such that after pinching the vertices $(u_i,v_i)$, the vertices $u_j$ and $v_j$ end up in two different blue faces.
elements.sent-2411-667: We resolve this issue in two different ways, depending on whether $(u_i,v_i)$ and $(u_j,v_j)$ are all in the same partite class or not:.
elements.sent-2411-668: If $u_i,v_i,u_j,v_j$ are all in the same partite class, then we replace the collection of pinchings $\{\pp_{u_i,v_i ,\pp_{u_j,v_j}\}$ with the collection of three pinchings\footnote{Note that $\pp_{u_i,v_i}$ is an arbitrary choice which could be replaced by $\pp_{u_j,v_j}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-669: If $(u_i,v_i)$ and $(u_j,v_j)$ are in opposite partite classes, then we say that the collection of pinchings $\{\pp_{u_i,v_i ,\pp_{u_j,v_j}\}$ is non feasible (see the third example in Figure~\ref{fig-pinching2 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-670: We say that a collection of pinchings $\cpp=\{\pp_{u_i,v_i}\}_{i=1 ^r$ is feasible if it does not contain any pair of non feasible pinchings.}
elements.sent-2411-671: Note that if a collection of pinchings $\cpp=\{\pp_{u_i,v_i}\}_{i=1 ^r$ is feasible then the individual pinchings can be performed in any order and always produce the same map.}
elements.sent-2411-672: Given a feasible collection of pinchings $\cpp$ of the blue face $B$, we often write $\cpp(M)$ to denote the embedded map obtained by performing the collection of pinchings $\cpp$ to the blue face $B$ of the embedded map $M$, and $\cpp(B)$ to denote the family of blue faces obtained by performing the collection of pinchings $\cpp$.
elements.sent-2411-673: Finally, let $\ACP(M,B)$ denote the set of all feasible collections of pinchings $\cpp$ of the blue face $B$ and $\AM(M,B)$ denote the set of all the embedded maps $\cpp(M)$ obtained from some collection of pinchings $\cpp\in\ACP(M,B)$ (where $\ACP$ stays for ``all pinchings" and $\AM$ for ``all maps").

elements.sent-2411-710: Note that since a (single) pinching of the vertices of the blue face $B$ creating a family of blue faces $B'$ cannot cause $B'$ to have non-distinct vertices, all the invalid collections of pinchings $\cpp\in\ICP(M,B)$ must create at least one enclosure loop in $\cpp(M)$ (recall the definition of enclosure loop from the paragraph below Definition~\ref{defn:non-separableplanar-embedded ).}
elements.sent-2411-711: We chose one of these enclosure loops and we assume that it is made of blue faces all sent by the embedding to the lattice edge $e$.
elements.sent-2411-712: Focusing on this enclosure loop, which we can assume to be a simple loop as remarked in Observation~\ref{obs:enclosure loops , and unpinching one by one all the vertices of $B$ in $\cpp(B)$ that were pinched by $\cpp$ (in an arbitrarily fixed order), we see that this simple loop will be split into a collections of arcs connecting pairs of vertices of $B$, all of them being in the same partite class.}

elements.sent-2411-715: From now on, we say that two vertices $u$ and $v$ of the same partite class on the boundary of $B$ are connected by an arc sent to $e$, if there exists a connected sequence of blue faces of $M$ all sent by the embedding to the lattice edge $e$ and connecting $u$ and $v$.

elements.sent-2411-717: Next we introduce a useful definition.
elements.sent-2411-718: Given two vertices $u$ and $v$ on a blue face $B$ we define the vertices between $u$ and $v$ to be the vertices on $B$ that lie between $u$ and $v$ when going around the boundary of $B$ counter-clockwise starting from $u$ and ending at $v$.
elements.sent-2411-719: With this, we are able to state the following property about maps with invalid pinchings which will be crucial to prove Theorem~\ref{thm:master-sum-blue-faces .}
elements.sent-2411-720: Consider $M\in\npe(\ell,K)$ and one of its blue faces $B$.

elements.sent-2411-723: Let $u_1$ and $v_1$ be two such vertices.
elements.sent-2411-724: Now if all the vertices between $u_1$ and $v_1$ of the opposite partite class are not connected by an arc to another vertex of $B$ then we are done.

elements.sent-2411-726: First notice $z$ cannot be between $v_1$ and $u_1$.
elements.sent-2411-727: This is because, if it was the case, then the arc connecting $w$ and $z$ must intersect the arc connecting $u_1$ and $v_1$ and this is not possible.
